findings.description: Logo graphic missing from the HTML cover page.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF shows the SAMHSA logo in the upper-right corner; the HTML rendering does not display this logo and only shows text content.

findings.description: Cover-page title styling is not fully preserved.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF uses larger, bold blue title text and a bold blue subtitle; the HTML renders the same text in darker/black styling with less visual distinction.

findings.description: The cover page present in the PDF is missing as a distinct section/page in the HTML; its contents are merged into the body rather than preserved as the first page/section.
findings.evidence: PDF page 1 is a standalone cover with the SAMHSA logo, 'Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) Unified Performance Reporting Tool (SUPRT) - C', 'YOUTH (12 TO 17) / CLIENT / REASSESSMENT FORM', and 'Version: June 2025'. In HTML, these cover elements appear at the start of the document, but there is no separate first-page section corresponding to the PDF cover; the HTML immediately continues into the next page content after an <hr>.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is not faithful to the document structure: multiple top-level document sections are encoded as h3 instead of maintaining a consistent hierarchy under the single h1/h2 title.
findings.evidence: HTML uses h1 for the document title and h2 for 'YOUTH (12 TO 17) / CLIENT / REASSESSMENT FORM', then uses h3 for 'CLIENT CONSENT', 'A. SOCIAL DRIVERS OF HEALTH', 'Thank you for completing this reassessment form.', and '[OFFICE USE ONLY] RECORD MANAGEMENT – YOUTH / CLIENT / REASSESSMENT'. In the PDF, these are major section headers at comparable structural level, not subordinate subsection headings beneath an h2 subtitle.

findings.description: The HTML contains duplicated top-level main container elements, introducing unnecessary structural duplication.
findings.evidence: HTML shows nested '<main><main> ... </main></main>'. This duplicates the primary content wrapper even though the PDF has only one continuous document structure.
